schools.summary: The body a 'tabernacle/tent,' 'dissolved' (katalythe, the word for striking down a tent), set against 'a building of God... not made with hands, eternal in the heavens'; the confident 'we know' the ground of Paul's calm amid affliction.
Tertullian (against Marcion): the 'eternal home in heaven' does not mean the body, built by the Creator, perishes forever.

In the tent 'we groan' (cf Rom 8:23), 'earnestly desiring to be clothed upon' (ependysasthai, to put on over oneself like an upper garment) with the heavenly house; the groaning from the body's burdens and a forward longing for glory, the mortal swallowed of life.

A much-interpreted clause (Barnes: read many ways); 'naked' (gymnoi) the disembodied spirit stripped of its covering; the believer's hope not to remain a bare spirit but to be 'clothed' with the heavenly body, 'not found naked'; the particles expressing confident expectation (Ellicott).
Calvin restricts the glorious clothing to believers — the wicked too are stripped of the body but appear in shameful nakedness.

Groaning 'burdened' (the body a weight, cf Wisdom 9:15), yet not wishing 'to be unclothed' (a bare disembodied spirit) but 'clothed upon,' 'that mortality might be swallowed up of life' — the hope of bodily glorification, not dissolution; faith rejects the philosophy that seeks mere disembodiment (Wesley).

God 'hath wrought us' (fashioned, fitted us) for this very thing — the heavenly glory; the longing supernatural, not natural (Calvin); and he gave 'the earnest of the Spirit,' the Holy Spirit as a down-payment and pledge guaranteeing the full inheritance.

'Always confident' (of good courage, full of hope, not dejected); yet 'at home in the body' (endemountes) means 'absent from the Lord' — present bodily life keeping the believer at a distance from Christ's immediate presence; the courage a gift of the Spirit (Calvin).

'Walk' the whole conduct of life (the Christian a pilgrim); 'by faith, not by sight' — rather 'not by appearance' (eidos = the form/fashion of things seen), but by trust in the unseen realities promised; faith itself a kind of seeing of the invisible, beneath the open sight to come.

'Willing rather' (eudokoumen, we prefer, are well-pleased) 'to be absent from the body and present with the Lord' (cf Phil 1:23) — at death the soul in immediate, conscious nearness to Christ, blessedness not wholly deferred to the resurrection (Wesley); he accepts even the 'unclothed' state because it brings him to the Lord (Ellicott).

'We make it our aim' (philotimoumetha, holy ambition, fond of honour) — the one worthy Christian ambition, to be 'acceptable to him' whether present or absent; the sure hope of heaven breeding not sloth but diligence (Henry).

'We must all' (apostles and all) be 'made manifest' before the 'judgment seat' (bematos) of Christ — Saviour, Lord, and Judge; each 'receiving' according to the deeds done in the body, good or bad; not overthrowing grace, but the life made manifest; Paul kept himself before this bar (Calvin).

'The terror' better 'the fear of the Lord' (reverential awe, not slavish dread, born of v10); it propels rather than paralyses — 'we persuade men' (conative, try to persuade); his life 'made manifest unto God' and, he trusts, to their consciences, against his detractors.
Chrysostom: knowing that terrible judgment seat, Paul does everything with watchful strictness to give no offence.

Not commending himself again but giving them 'occasion of glorying' (aphormen, a base/material) on his behalf, that they may answer those who 'glory in appearance, not in heart' (the false teachers boasting in outward show); his self-defence for their sake, not vanity (Henry).

Answering a sneer that he was 'beside himself' (exestemen, ecstasy; cf Festus' taunt) — his fervour/visions 'to God' (devotion God understands), his 'sober' plain ministry 'for your cause'; both springing from a selfless source, directed to God and their good, never self-reputation.

'The love of Christ constraineth' (synechei, holds together, presses on every side, bears along like wind and tide); 'if one died for all, then were all dead' — his death for all proving all were dead in sin and involving them representatively; the master-motive of Paul's life.

debates.question: Is 'the love of Christ' his love for us or our love for him?

sides.position: Christ's love for us (his dying love, v15) — Paul's uniform usage decisive
includes also the believer's answering love to Christ; the two intertwined

schools.summary: Christ died for all 'that they which live should not henceforth live unto themselves, but unto him which died and rose' — the atonement claiming the whole life; bought with his blood, they are not their own, selfishness ruled out, owed wholly to the risen Lord.

themes: but unto him who died and rose

schools.summary: To 'know after the flesh' (kata sarka) is to value by worldly standards (descent, rank, wealth — what the false teachers prized); living unto Christ, Paul esteems men so no more; even a merely earthly conception of Christ (the Jewish temporal Messiah) he has abandoned for the crucified and risen Lord.

themes: know no man after the flesh
Christ known no more after the flesh

schools.summary: 'In Christ' (united by faith), one is 'a new creature/creation' (kaine ktisis, a fresh making) — a creative act of God, for only the power that makes a world can make a Christian (Wesley); 'old things passed away' (gone like snow in spring), 'all things become new'; the great statement of the new birth.

'All things of God' — the new creation flowing from grace, not man; God 'reconciled us to himself by Jesus Christ' (katallaxantos, changing from enmity), the Reconciler taking the initiative; and gave 'the ministry of reconciliation,' entrusting the proclamation to his ministers.

The gospel compressed: 'God was in Christ reconciling the world unto himself' (God's work, in Christ, embracing sinners), 'not imputing their trespasses' (forgiveness by the non-reckoning of sin, grounded in v21), and 'committed unto us the word of reconciliation' (the message entrusted to ministers).

'Ambassadors for Christ' (presbeuomen, the Emperor's Legate) — on his behalf and in his stead (Eph 6:20), bearing his authority; the astonishing condescension of the offended God beseeching the rebels through them; 'be ye reconciled to God' — man laying down his enmity to accept the reconciliation freely offered.

The great exchange — the sinless Christ ('who knew no sin,' a definite claim of his sinlessness, 1 Pet 2:22) 'made to be sin for us,' that 'we might be made the righteousness of God in him'; our sin laid on him, his righteousness reckoned to us; no return to favour except founded on this sacrifice alone (Calvin).

debates.question: What does 'made him to be sin' mean?

sides.position: made a sin-offering, the sacrifice for sin
the imputation of our guilt to the sinless One, treated as sin in our place (both senses combine)

themes: 'made him to be sin for us'
the righteousness of God in him
